slide_text:
SDG Strategies

Strategy 1
**End-to-End Generation**
- Generation of complete <query, positive, negatives> text triplets from scratch.
  - First step is to generate tasks and topics. Second step is to generate data.

Strategy 2
**Seed Corpus Expansion**
- Use existing seed documents corpus to generate synthetic queries.
  - Mine hard negatives from the same corpus.

Strategy 3
**Dataset Translation**
- Translate existing high-quality datasets into other languages.

transcript_segments.text: And the third strategy is a simple translation.
So again, there are some high-quality data sets in, let's say, only English.
And we'd like to make sure that our model generalizes to other languages and it is multilingual.
That is why it translates some of these high-quality data sets towards other languages.

transcript_segments.text: So here is a simple table where I compare these two approaches.
So firstly, we'll discuss the indexing speed.
So indexing, it is the step where you need to encode all your documents and store your embeddings for your documents in the vector database.
So we already discussed that in their text case, it might be slower because you need to extract tables, charts, do OCR, while in the visual case, it just like split images.
However, the indexing model is different.
So in the text case, you can just use LLM, while for images, you should use the LLM model, so vision language model.
And vision language models are pretty slow, especially if you'd like to work with high-definition PDF images with smaller text, and you'd like to enlarge them even further.
And that is why the index and speed is not necessarily faster and simpler with this visual approach, right?
And text might be kind of comparable on that, and it depends on your data and your use case, which one would work better and faster for you.
Right, well, on the querying side, they are similar issues.
So let's say if our input queries are only text, then each of the models, each of the pipelines take only LLM part of it, and you pass this through LLM, and the latency here is the same.
And overall, for the performance metrics for the actual retrieval accuracy, our recommendation is if you have some text-rich documents, you should rely on the text pipeline.
If you have some visual-rich documents with a lot of infographics, charts, and tables, you should rely on their visual paths.
But to get the best out of the world, actually the best approach is to combine them two.
And use VLM as your Ibanic model, but instead of passing only their pages of the PDFs here, you can also pass text here.
So you can pass text and pages to your VLM as to inputs, and then it produces the best result.
And it works better than the text and the visual, but of course it is the most expensive method because you need to process first the text and then use VLM for indexing.

transcript_segments.text: And to build this model, we basically use the same techniques.
So as architecture here, as a backbone, we are using Quen3VL8B model.

So latent interaction is a technique that allows you to find more fine-grained similarities between your queries and your documents.

Because in the usual case, when we look at the classic architecture of bind quarter here on the left, we pass our queries through the LLM, we pass our document either through VLLM or VLLM, and then we do the global pooling.

transcript_segments.text: Because in the usual case, when we look at the classic architecture of bind quarter here on the left, we pass our queries through the LLM, we pass our document either through VLLM or VLLM, and then we do the global pooling.
And after this global pooling, we kind of lose this fine-grained control of what's encoded into this model.
And that is why we have only single vector representation for each of the queries in the document.

While here on the right is a latent direction architecture, where after passing our queries through the LLM, we actually take individual tokens and we find the similarity between these token embeddings with all the tokens in our document.
So basically, we make this like many to many kind of combination, and we take the maximum similarity between each token and all the tokens in the document,
with the idea that potentially we might find some fine-grained relations between the query in this long document instead of just kind of storing them in the single vector.
And the pipeline with VLM might look something like this.

Trade-off: Retrieval Accuracy vs. Storage

Model Family
- 1B single vector

transcript_segments.text: And here in the chart, we compare this kind of performance versus the storage costs.
So the y-axis is the NDC geometric, basically accuracy of your retrieval.
The x-axis is how many gigabytes you need to store 1 million pages from the index.
And our baseline here is bottom left, our 1B model, single vector.
And you can see that with this state-of-the-art lead interaction models, we can get pretty nice boost in the metrics.
But also, the storage cost grows a lot.
And it's kind of like a factor of 100.
So you need hundreds more times storage to actually store these vectors.
And this is like active area of research, how you can compress these vectors.
So you can kind of like slice them, you can quantize them, you can pull them or cluster them.
There are different ways you can suppress this, but overall still the storage will be more expensive compared to these like single vector approaches.

transcript_segments.text: Another active direction of research right now is agentic retrieval because, again, everyone is talking about agents.
It might be like agentic search, agentic retrieval.
And we also published a blog post on Huggingface about it and about our approach.
And this specific work is more important for queries that are not that simple as we are like you used to do.
So it might be some multi-hop queries or multiple questions.
It might require some different parts of your databases and so on.
And that is why once you have your query, you have LLM agent that decides what to do with it.
And we provided just like three simple tools to it.
So the first tool is basically think.
So it can think about this query.
It can decide to rewrite it, maybe come up with different queries, split it into multiple questions.
Then it has a tool for the retrieval.
So basically given the prompt, retrieve back the results.
So here is where our retrieval model is running.
And then it can make a loop and make it run until it's happy with the result.
Once it is happy with the results, it can go ahead and select the last tool, which selects the relevant content based on these discoveries and returns back the results.
So this approach looks pretty nice and works pretty well.
But of course, it is much more expensive compared to just using a single query and single retrieval call as old approaches.

Search is business critical
Entire team that only handle search results quality

Overview

1. Search Query
   - user input

2. Full-text search

2. Semantic search

3. Candidate set

   - freshness
   - authority

5. Top 10
   - shown or passed to LLM

   - semantic similarity
   - recency
   - authority / quality

slide_text:
Competing Signals

Fulltext search vs Semantic Search
Which one should have priority for your dataset

Recall vs Precision
Fewer missed answers or cleaner top results

Ranking
Multiple scoring functions can influence the order of the results (freshness, popularity)

Quality vs Latency
Users do not want to wait long for their search response.

transcript_segments.text: And you'll have competing signals here.
So which one do you want to use more?
The full text one or the semantic one?
Do you have like concrete thing that you're searching for or user base that is searching for special keys?
Or do you have like vague queries that you just want to have the results for?
Do you want to maximize recall or precision?
Because for users, you would maybe want to maximize precision because he wants to have as much relevant documents on top as possible.
The agent could read 30 more items without any worries.
You still want to have it as good as possible because one of the documents could have something
important for answering the question, but he can read more queries, more of the documents to answer the document than the user.
Then there's ranking, so the freshness or popularity of the items, and quality versus latency.
So you want to answer as soon as possible to the user, and you don't want to wait for the answer of the LLM.

slide_text:
TF-IDF

- Term Frequency: terms appearing more often in a document are more important for that document.
- Inverse Document Frequency: terms appearing in many documents are less useful
- Final score rewards documents that contain query terms often, especially if those terms are rare in the corpus.

Problems
- Repeated terms overboost
- Long documents have advantage

tf(t, d) = (f_d(t)) / (max_wed f_d(w))

idf(t, D) = ln(|D| / |{d ∈ D : t ∈ d}|)

tfidf(t, d, D) = tf(t, d) * idf(t, D)

tfidf'(t, d, D) = (idf(t, D) / |D|) + tfidf(t, d, D)

f_d(t) := frequency of term t in document d
D := corpus of documents

slide_text:
BM25

- BM25 scores documents by query term rarity, term frequency, and document length
- Intuition: BM25 is TF-IDF with term-frequency saturation and document-length normalization.

Parameters

Parameter    | Controls               | Typical Tuning Range | Intuition
-------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------
k1           | Term-frequency saturation | 0.5-2.5              | How much repeated terms help
b            | Length normalization    | 0-1.0                | long documents penalization factor

transcript_segments.text: So the answer for that is BN25.
It has two parameters.
You have the term frequency saturation and the length normalization.
So then you would just tune this parameter and maybe say how much the repeated terms help or how the long documents that you have in your data set should be penalized.

transcript_text: So the answer for that is BN25. It has two parameters. You have the term frequency saturation and the length normalization. So then you would just tune this parameter and maybe say how much the repeated terms help or how the long documents that you have in your data set should be penalized.

slide_text:
Where BM25 falls short

Query: car insurance
Relevant doc: "auto coverage policy"
→ BM25 misses synonyms.

Query: laptop battery dies quickly
Relevant doc: "device loses charge after"
→ Same meaning, different wording.

Query: GDPR deletion request
Relevant doc: "right to erasure under Article 17"
→ Domain terminology mismatch.

Query: how to cancel my account
Relevant doc: "subscription termination process"
→ User language vs internal language.

slide_text:
Embeddings

- Language model embeddings
- Embed the query & docs in same vector space
- Retrieve the KNN
- Similar meaning can match without token overlap
- Users ask short vague or paraphrased questions
- Cost - latency

Query: how to cancel my account

Embeddings rank by meaning, not just shared words.

Embedding space (2D projection)
- how to cancel my account
- subscription termination process (nearest)
- close workspace and remove members (medium distance)
- account billing settings (farther)
- cancel pending invoice (farther)

slide_text:
Latency & HNSW

- Hierarchical Navigable Small World
- Querying a vector database takes too long
- Because of the dimensionality common structures like M-Tree pruning stops working
- Only approximate (stuck in local minimum)
- Good recall latency trade off

transcript_segments.text: So can we at least solve the latency?
Yes, we can.
And that's with the hierarchical and navigable small world.
The idea is that you'll have these layers.
And on the top, totally bottom layer, you would have all the points or all the documents here.
And the higher you would go up, the less documents you would have.
And you go with this greedy search, finding some good local minimum in each layer.
and going over until you found a good local minimum in the totally bottom layer.
So you wouldn't get precise results, but you would get them fast and you would still get a really good recall off of it.

transcript_text: So can we at least solve the latency? Yes, we can. And that's with the hierarchical and navigable small world. The idea is that you'll have these layers. And on the top, totally bottom layer, you would have all the points or all the documents here. And the higher you would go up, the less documents you would have. And you go with this greedy search, finding some good local minimum in each layer. and going over until you found a good local minimum in the totally bottom layer. So you wouldn't get precise results, but you would get them fast and you would still get a really good recall off of it.

slide_text:
HNSW Parameters

Parameter       | Stage          | Typical tuning range | Tradeoff
--------------- | -------------- | --------------------- | ---------------------------
M              | index build   | 8-64                 | graph degree, memory, recall ceiling
efConstruction | index build   | 50-500               | build time vs graph quality
efSearch       | query time    | 20-1000              | latency vs recall
dimensions     | index/query   | model-defined, often 256-3072 | quality vs memory and latency

Goal           | M      | efConstruction | efSearch
-------------- | ------ | -------------- | --------
Lower latency (baseline) | 4      | 400            | 100-500
Higher recall           | 6-8    | 600-800        | 700-1000
Max recall experiment   | 8-10   | 800-1000       | 1000

So the higher you would go with the EF search, the better recall you would have, but there's a cost on the latency.

slide_text:
Hybrid Search

- BM25 catches exact lexical evidence
- Vector search catches semantic evidence
- Fusion turns multiple ranked lists into one candidate set
- Ranking decides the final order
- How are the sets merged when having different ranges
  - Cosine distance [-1, 1]
  - BM25 [0, x] where x >= 0

transcript_segments.text: All right, so when this comes together...
you would have results from the full text search or the lexical list search, and then you would have the vector list search.
And how would you merge those two together?
Because the weights of the documents in both of those lists are totally different, and they mean different thing.
So here, the costing distance could be between minus 1 and 1, and the BM25 could be from 0 to any positive integer.
And so you need to somehow fusion them together.
And then that's where the reciprocal rank fusion comes in, RF.

transcript_text: All right, so when this comes together... you would have results from the full text search or the lexical list search, and then you would have the vector list search. And how would you merge those two together? Because the weights of the documents in both of those lists are totally different, and they mean different thing. So here, the costing distance could be between minus 1 and 1, and the BM25 could be from 0 to any positive integer. And so you need to somehow fusion them together. And then that's where the reciprocal rank fusion comes in, RF.

slide_text:
Reciprocal Rank Fusion

**[A] Standard RRF**
score(d) = Σ (1 / (k + rankᵢ(d)))
- Each retriever contributes equally.

**[B] Weighted RRF**
score(d) = Σ (wᵢ / (k + rankᵢ(d)))
- wᵢ = weight of retriever i
- Higher weight = more influence
- Uses ranks only, not raw scores

**[C] Two-set example**
BM25 results:
1 | D1
2 | D4
3 | D2

Vector results:
1 | D2
2 | D3
3 | D1

weights: BM25 = 0.7, Vector = 1.3 | k = 60
- D2 gets a strong fused score because it ranks in both lists and is #1 in Vector.

Fused top candidates: D2, D1, D3, D4

When to use
- Use weighting when one retriever is generally more reliable.

Common default
- Start with BM25 = 1.0 and Vector = 1.0

Tune
- Example: BM25 = 1.2, Vector = 0.8 when exact keyword match matters more.

Parameter k
- Typical default: k = 60

transcript_segments.text: And then that's where the reciprocal rank fusion comes in, RF.
And what you do is you wouldn't take the weights off of the both sorted list, you would just take their order, the closeness to the query.
And what you do is take some parameter k, and the higher the k is, the less influence the top-ranked results would have.
And then, well, how you can see here, you would just retrieve from the BM25, the D1, D4, and D2 documents here, the D2, D3, and D1 document.
And you would just fuse them together using this formula.
We just go over the both sets and get some rank.
off of them, and then you would have fused candidates that goes from D2 to D4 based on the relevancy.
And again, you can tune the K parameter, and also you can tune how big of a weight should order of the BM25 result have to the vector result.
So another parameter is to tune.

slide_text:
Metrics

- Recall@K: relevant docs are present
  - RAG candidate retrieval
- Precision@K: top K are relevant
  - user-facing search
- NDCG@K: graded relevance and order
  - Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain
  - ranked search quality
- MRR@K: how high is the first relevant result
  - known item lookup

- Latency@K: not a quality metric but should be taken into account
  - ensemble metric with those above

It's going to cumulative gain.
And it takes into account both the relevancy of the document and the order of the document.

And then also not a quality metric, but you want to have the latency as low as possible.

slide_text:
NDCG

1 **Assign relevance labels**
Each result is judged with a relevance label from 0 to 2.

```
Rank   Result   Rel (0–2)
1      Result A  2
2      Result B  1
3      Result C  2
4      Result D  0
...    ...      ...
```

Relevance legend (intuition)
2 = exact answer; should rank at the top
1 = somewhat relevant; acceptable lower in ranking
0 = not useful

2 **Map relevance to gain**
Relevance labels are converted into numeric gain.

gain(rel) = 2^rel − 1

Gain converts these usefulness judgments into numeric value.

Common mapping
rel | 0 | 1 | 2
gain(rel) | 0 | 1 | 3

Gain function can be customized to your needs.

3 **Compute discounted gain**
Discount gains by rank position. Earlier ranks matter more.

DCG@K = Σ (from i=1 to K) gain(rel_i) / log₂(i + 1)

Rank: 1  2  3  4  5 ...
Gain:  [high bar] [medium bar] [medium bar] [low bar] [low bar] ...

Higher ranks get less discount (position discount)

4 **Normalize by the ideal ranking**
NDCG is the DCG of the ideal ordering (sorted by rel. desc.).

NDCG@K = DCG@K of the ranking / IDCG@K

IDCG@K = DCG@K of the ideal ranking.

Range 0.0–1.0, higher is better

NDCG measures whether the most useful results appear near the top.

   k1=1.0, eFSearch=150, RRF k=40, topN=200
   discarded

Experiment on your own data.

Search strategies
- grid search: few parameters, cheap eval
- random search: many parameters, uneven importance
- Bayesian optimization: expensive trials, 4+ meaningful parameters
- multi-objective search: quality and latency both matter

Narrow / sample next trials

Not every technical addition wins
- embeddings can reduce exact-match quality
- reranking can improve precision but miss latency budgets
- freshness boosts can bury evergreen runbooks
- popularity can reinforce already-visible documents

slide_text:
Overfitting

**Common failure**
Be careful about overfitting (overconfiguring)
- Many tunable parameters
  e.g., BM25 k1/b, vector topK, efSearch, RRF k, weights, boosts, reranker top-N...
- Many trials and tweaks
  trying lots of combinations and adjustments
- Repeatedly checking metrics
  peeking at results during development
- Result: offline gain that disappears after launch

**How overfitting happens**
Used for exploration
Used for selection
Used for final estimate
- Tuning queries → Validation queries → Holdout test queries
  Try many configurations
  Compare finalists
  Touch once at the end
- Principle: Separate exploration, selection, and final estimation. Don't optimize the benchmark—optimize the search experience.
- Only one final check

**Safer setup practices**
| Practice                     | Why it matters                                                                                     |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Train/dev/test split         | Keeps the final estimate honest                                                                     |
| Temporal split               | Avoids learning future behavior                                                                     |
| Head/torso/tail coverage     | Prevents optimizing only frequent queries                                                         |
| Deduplicate near-identical queries | Avoids leakage and inflated metrics                                                      |
| Freeze test set access       | Prevents metric shopping                                                                           |

**Same discipline as model training**
- Tuning (exploration) set
  Choose and tune search parameters
- Validation (selection) set
  Compare candidate configurations
- Holdout test set
  Final estimate, touched as little as possible

**Use temporal split to avoid learning future behavior**
- Older queries → More recent queries → Newest queries (protected)
  - Tuning queries: Explore many configurations
  - Validation queries: Select the best configuration
  - Holdout test queries: Final estimate only
- Do not let information flow from right to left

slide_text:
Observability

Search Observability (Production)

SIGNAL      SOURCE      WHAT IT CAN REVEAL
🔍 CTR      🔍 search bar      results look useful enough to click
📊 click position      🔍 search bar      ranking quality in the top results
🚫 zero-click rate      🔍 search bar      likely unsatisfied searches
🔄 reformulation within 30s      🔍 search bar or chatbot      user had to ask differently
📄 retrieved chunks and citations      🤖 chatbot / RAG      whether the answer had usable evidence
🔄 tool retries      🤖 agent      whether first-pass retrieval was weak
⏱ dwell time / return rate      🔍 search bar      clicked result did or did not satisfy
📈 p50 / p95 / p99 latency by stage      🔍 both      where the budget is going

Quality signals = user outcomes
Mechanics signals = system performance
We monitor both

Failure analysis
zero-click      12.4%
reformulated      9.1%
clicked low rank      6.7%
no citation      8.3%
high latency      7.2%

Example hard case added
🔍 invoice OCR returns empty vendor
Annotations:
🚫 Zero-click
📄 No citation
🔍 Root cause: OCR tool miss

Add hard cases to eval
Capture raw interactions & annotations

Deploy / experiment
Retune hyperparameters
Roll out changes behind a flag or in a canary

Monitor both quality and mechanics. Use production failures to grow the eval set and improve search.

slide_text:
Azure AI Search

**[Concept]**
1. lexical retrieval
2. vector retrieval
3. hybrid retrieval
4. fusion
5. vector weighting
6. recency/popularity boosts
7. reranking
8. embeddings

**[Azure AI Search / OpenAI capability]**
- full-text search scored with BM25
- vector fields with HNSW or exhaustive KNN
- text query plus "vectorQueries" in one request
- RRF for parallel result sets
- vector query weights
- scoring profiles with freshness / magnitude functions
- semantic ranker, reported separately as "@search.rerankerScore"
- Azure OpenAI / OpenAI "text-embedding-3-*" models

**[Replaceable pieces]**
- search engine
- embedding model
- reranker
- monitoring

These parts can be swapped while keeping the retrieval architecture pattern.

(1) Concrete Azure/OpenAI example; concepts remain portable.

slide_text:
Takeaway

- Search is not just embedding search
- Upon search performing badly you can try
  - BM25 and embeddings have complementary failure modes
  - RRF is a good starting point; labels let you tune
  - On wrong order of the search results add ranking

- Experiment on your own data; not every added signal improves search
- Guard against eval overfitting
- Keep the system measurable and replaceable

slide_text:
The tool half-life

Emplifi
- Cursor
  - pilot in 2025,
  - massive trainings in Q1/26
  - Main IDE for engineers

- Q2 2026
  - Claude Code / Codex and other already in discussion

Industry
(Pragmatic Engineer, 2026)
- Claude Code
  - 0 -> 1 AI Coding tool in 8 months

- 70% of engineers juggle 2-4 AI tools simultaneously

transcript_segments.text: And it's not just us.
If you take a look at the Pragmatic Engineer 2026 AI tooling survey, you can find it, for example, Cloud Code went from zero to number one within a couple of months, eight months, I believe.
And it also looks that 70% of engineers regularly use two to four different tools simultaneously.
So nobody is standardizing on one tool.
As I said, tools have six months half-life, but strategies you are building shouldn't.
If you put your bet on the specific IDE, you will probably need to change your bet every six months.
Another thing is that vendors can easily change their terms underneath.
We've seen really brutal changes in limits of the tokens last month.
Some of the alternative have so insane terms and conditions that your legal team basically won't allow you to use them.
So I would say the five phases I'm going to talk more are designed to be more portable, whatever tool wins, because tools can change your strategy and operating model shouldn't.

slide_text:
Phase 2 – trust layer
Do not skip

- Agent Identity: Every agent separate from humans, with its own scoped credentials
- Behavioral Boundaries: Policy-as-code
- Data Scoping: Real-time data boundaries, least privilege, PII segmentation
- Audit Trail: Centralized logs, end2end tracing
- Kill Switches: Real-time revocation

Owner:
Security + Platform

Done when:
Every agent action routes through the gateway

Failure when:
We’ll add governance later.*
* you won’t

slide_text:
Phase 2 – the AI gateway

- Monitors all traffic – who called what, when, for whom
- Enforces policy, identity, rate limits, dev vs prod isolation
- Cost reporting by model, team, workflow – not just total spend
- Tracks tokens cost per workflow as unit cost (not productivity)
- Handles the product + internal blend (control for both)

Boring infra.
The single most important piece you could build.

slide_text:
10 Items Checklist

1. Inventory internal APIs, rank by agent potential
2. Convert top 5–10 services to MCP (schema + perms) *
3. Per-agent identity (separate from human users; scoped credentials) *
4. Trust gateway (policy + audit) BEFORE scaling *
5. Start with 3 automations: PR reviews, tests, docs
6. Measure from day 1: resolution, defects, FP, SAT, regressions
7. After 90 days, use DATA to justify multi-agent
8. Handoff protocols: full context + human checkpoints
9. Redefine roles & ladders *
10. Replace output metrics with outcome metrics

We have measured:

UX/UI creates a framework that enables users to truly leverage the potential of LLM tools while also providing relevant data for measurement.

UX also involves the frequency with which we query users (too often = annoying => low output quality).

We maximize UX when working with AI, which results in employees being satisfied with AI adoption. (our ENPS is +32 / ± 50% of employees truly supports AI).

2,18%
That’s the current measured productivity uplift.

5%
That’s our 2026 target.
We can rely on this only if we can maintain the integrity of productivity measurement at scale.

transcript_segments.text: Good afternoon.
My name is Jonas Krathochville and I work in a company called Fall Wine.
Together with our teams we are building AI systems for legal professionals.
Today I want to talk about how we approach evaluations and talk about why a simple benchmark is often not enough in our domain and about the framework we have to overcome this.
As it turns out, generating the answer is not the hard part.
The hard part is generating the answer that's actually useful for real-world legal professionals.
Our field is very subjective, and there's often no single ground truth.
Also, the workflows are quite high stakes, so we need to be careful what we present to our users.
So the way how we try to approach this is to break the system down into individual levels and try to match the evaluation criteria to each of these levels.

transcript_segments.text: So the system we are actually building and trying to evaluate is something we call LOIS.
It stands for Legal Operating Intelligent System.
It's a multi-agentic system through which the attorney can either retrieve information or it can take action on behalf of them in the following ecosystem.
But what I really want to focus on is what we call this knowledge engine.
This is the part.
This is the system that's actually responsible for ingesting all those data and reaching them and making them queryable.
Some challenges that we have here is the scale of Valvine.
We have around 200 million pages worth of documents uploaded every day to our platform.
And a single case can have something between 100 to 1,000 of such documents.
So the retrieval and the context management for this system is very big challenge.
Also, we deal with this vast diversity of data inputs.
And everything we produce needs to be verifiable to the user, because as I said earlier, we need to show the proof that's actually what we're generating is grounded in some source material.
So to maybe give you a practical example of queries we can encounter in a wild, here is one such query.

On the vertical axis, we have basically information about where the evaluation signal comes from.
We have our in-house domain experts that would be our legal or parallel teams who are the domain experts who understand how the outputs should look like.
We also have our in-house data annotators team that have the bandwidth and capacity to digest the cases end to end.
And also, we gather feedback from real
production usage, together with the associated metadata that we can use.
On the horizontal axis, we see at the bot level of the system, these evaluations are most useful.
So we have the answer level telling us if the answer is good or bad.
Case level is basically giving us information about if the system understands the case as a whole.
And population level reveals some systematic errors.
And the diagonal basically conveys the idea where each of those evaluation levels are best paired with the level of the system.
So experts are best on the answer level where they can evaluate things like style, relevance, hallucinations, or citation coverage.
The annotators are really good in this enumeration and completeness.

Answer-level: what experts can judge directly

"List all treatments the plaintiff received from Dr. Reyes between the initial ER visit and the IME on August 5 that were causally related to the lumbar disc herniation."

Answer A

Dr. Reyes administered the following treatments between the 03/12 ER visit and the 08/05 IME: (1) cervical epidural steroid injection on 04/18 [1], (2) lumbar discectomy consultation on 05/22 [2], (3) physical therapy referral, 12 sessions starting 06/01 [3]. All treatments were related to the L4-L5 disc herniation diagnosed on 03/15.

Sources: [1] Reyes treatment notes 04/18, [2] surgical consult 05/22, [3] PT referral 06/01

Answer B

The plaintiff received several treatments from Dr. Reyes including injections, physical therapy, and a surgery consultation. These were for a back injury sustained in the accident.

Sources: medical records

transcript_segments.text: This is expensive, it's nonscalable.
So how do we try to scale it?
We have basically two options in our use case.
The first one is through synthetic cases.
So we went, we put quite a lot of effort into developing
developing a system that we call a case synthesizer, which basically goal is to create full artificial cases containing all those different data types.
And the big advantage there is that we have full control over the information we are placing into those cases during the generation process.
We can parameterize the system, set up
how large the case should be, what complexity of documents we want, etc.
Basically design perfect cases with 100% of control over the information in contains.
So this is great scalable way, but it has the obvious disadvantage of being a synthetic data.
So the second way we have is more specific to us as a follow-on.
We have a large database of already closed cases.
We have around 30 million of already closed cases.
It turns out that
Our attorneys, they're manually extracting a lot of the important information for them, and they're inserting them into the system.
So we have these so-called custom sections.
So the attorney would name the section, for example, list of medical providers, of timeline of key events, et cetera.
And we can take the schema of the section.
We can reverse engineer.
possible queries that should lead to this question and then run the query through the system and compare the results if they match what the attorney already extracted manually into this section.
This gives us sort of like a silver bullet target that we can use for this completeness at scale.
So now we have the answer level, we have the case level, but it doesn't really tell us about the prioritization of the work.

transcript_segments.text: So now we have the answer level, we have the case level, but it doesn't really tell us about the prioritization of the work.
And this is the last way how we look at the evaluations, and it's through this slicing of our domains.
And basically what the slicing means is that we can look
We can basically try to find these more granular tiles that reveal us where the system actually is underperforming and where it's behaving well.
Because if we hide the performance under some generic average, it doesn't tell us much of a story.
But if we slice it, for example, on level of the query type, because the queries
can be quite diverse.
There are the search type of queries, more like needle in a haystack finding of information, all the way to summarize my case through filtering type of queries such as what we saw.
when the client wants to list the treatments, etc.
We also can slice the domain by legal practice area because we have many different practice areas that our clients work in such as personal injury, family law, etc.
And each of those are very different
data distributions, and query distributions.

slide_text:
Evaluation is how we decide what to build next

1. Slice performance
   Practice area x query type x evidence type x user role

2. Collect annotations
   Experts on answer quality, annotators on case completeness

3. Identify gaps
   Find the weak segments that averages hide

4. Prioritize R&D
   Feed back into engineering, improve, re-evaluate

The right feedback loops, at the right level of evaluation
